item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely beautiful!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to see some more to this, such as who is in those satellites, who responded with "Oh Shit" at the end, what happened to him? What did he realise?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With the introduction of the SCPS, wouldn't it be possible to go there now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this SCP, and want to see it expanded upon. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any other good space ones you can suggest?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a big fan of this one, so much so that do wish there was more. I like how you can figure out the story from the log, just from those last lines (the electronic verification and the "oh shit").

So, am I to take it that SCP-1396-1 is aware that they are in an alternate time/place, but are need to keep their presence a secret?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much enjoyed this one. It tells just enough to give a good story while also leaving room for speculation and interpretation.

Also, I have noticed a few comments suggest that SCP-1396-1 is an AI. Did I miss something? Because I don't get where in the article it is supposed to be implied.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this idea. Space scips are really appealing to me, and the thought of this just makes me a bit giddy. It's a solidly written article about an absolutely awesome idea. I heartily +1 this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1396-1 consists of a central hull coated with hexagonal ablative armor plates

"I AM ABLATIVE ARMOR! LIFE IS BORING, THEN BRIEFLY EXCITING, THEN OVER! I AM ABLATIVE ARMOR..."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"…projectiles of an unknown shape and composition…"
An energy weapon would make a lot more sense for even being able to escape Jupiter's insane atmosphere and gravity in order to strike at orbit, but this phrasing implies a physical weapon. To make that sort of determination, the probe would have had to gotten a pretty good look at the counterattack; enough that at least an estimate of speed and mass (relative to the impacted target) would be called for.

While I'm thinking on the probe's imaging capabilities, how did The Foundation figure out that the rods where specifically tungsten?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True enough. But having the last stat calmly ask for more ammo and reinforcements might be a novel idea to add some extra dread. It doesn't have to be "omg immediate ammo needed!" but hinting that its job isn't done yet would make the Foundation more aware and concerned that it may need to act.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it very much. One (probably worthless) suggestion, if you wanted to do the whole real-world even tie-in, you could un-redact the date for the final bombardment event to have it coincide with comet Shoemaker-Levy 9 breaking apart and striking Jupiter in July 1994 (or even the 2009 impact event that caused a black spot the size of the Pacific Ocean to appear near the planet's south pole).

Oh, and one other thing. It seems like the Foundation would be looking into any possible connection between this and SCP-364, would that be worth adding to this in some way?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find it funny to think what it thinks right after it realized thet it screwed with temporal shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't know why, but I've always had a soft spot for satellite articles, and this is a good one.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite like the maybe there still is a threat to present day Earth, maybe there isn't because the kill-sats got them all, thing here. Adding a "Oh, they are about to come kill us" would ruin that.

Also, the SCP articles don't necessarily take place in the same universe. No canon and all that. Maybe in this universe the Earth and Foundation are now fighting some alien race (that has destroyed the kill-sats and is kinetically bombarding the planet) for the survival of our species.

This article implies all that as one possibility without saying a word about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote, I just feel like there could be more. The sats should have some kind of unique mark that would encourage further investigation.

Oh! And have the sat requesting immediate reloading and reinforcement, 'cause "the bigs ones were waiting for us to run out" and they're coming up to the surface soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love all the implications in this. Great space skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed.
That said, spoiler giveout has sooort of its place when there's either no way for the Foundation to know something. Or when you fuck up trying to imply it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much enjoy space-based articles. +1

My headcanon is that this involves the future-humans from SCP-1342.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
@ #3:

This is precisely why I write the way I do and dislike giving out spoilers on a regular basis. When executed correctly, vagueness leads to wild speculation, which makes something entertaining for everyone, including the original author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

1) And now I'm wondering what happened to the alternate Foundation where their satellites disappeared.
3) There's actually a more intriguing scenario I thought of. The satellites ended in our reality, and ended up accidentally going after something absolutely *not* their intended target. Which was actually able to retaliate effectively, unlike the bugs. Remember, seeing through Jupiter's atmosphere is difficult - so their target picking was likely around the lines, "if there's something solid in there, it's one of the 'bugs'"
I think the last sat realized it right after he found out it's the wrong Foundation. That much more reason for "Oh shit!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you like it!

Spoilers!
Puff.
  • SCP-1396 consists of a cluster of networked, autonomous, artificially intelligent kill-sats launched by the Foundation in an alternate timeline (or perhaps the future, spacetime is funny like that) where our technology is somewhat more advanced. They were sent on a mission to hunt down a swarm of hostile "bugs" in Jupiter's atmosphere, where a kinetic bombardment weapon would be most effective, and somehow ended up in our plane of existence.
  • The AIs were either imprinted using human minds or were programmed by someone with an extremely twisted sense of humor, hence their weird, nonchalant attitude.
  • Whatever it was that the kill-sats were hunting was not only able to survive incredible atmospheric pressure on the scale of around 25 gigapascals (GPa), but was somehow able to nail multiple satellites with rocks (or quills, or whatever) at a distance of over 311 million kilometers when cornered and was probably responsible for displacing both itself and the kill-sats into our neighborhood to begin with. Are we sure the kill-sats actually killed their intended target?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eye feex.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but attempts to … remains unknown.

remain unsuccessful, perhaps?

Also, I like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for rods from God on Jupiter. Neat concept. I wish there was a little more info on what's going on with the battlestation operators and the backstory in general, but that's not always necessary with each article. +1, and I'm enjoying the fact that there have been a couple decent space SCPs this week.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I can't get Chrome, Firefox, or IE9 (either in default or in IE8/IE7 compatibility mode) to do that, but I'm just going to bite the bullet on this one and change it to the somewhat incorrect but more readable "L2".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mine looks like this: Not really sure if that's what you mean, Drewbear. I'm also on Firefox, if that helps.

e: I KNOW WHO YOU ARE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm at work, so I can't take/upload screenshots, but I'm using I.E. 8.0 (again: work. definitely not my preferred browser)

I hadn't thought about how the kinetic energy would remain the same even if it changed phases. Objection retracted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That subscript "2" in L2 is doing something wonky to the spacing that's bugging me.

Maybe, I pulled the subscript 2 from a relatively unexplored region of the Unicode standard, as I prefer to use character representations whenever possible because of the line-spacing issues caused by using super/subscript modifiers to HTML. If you could take a screenshot and let me know what browser you're using, that might help.

I'm not sure how well the rods would be able to survive penetrating that deep into the atmosphere. Also, Jupiter's liquid core doesn't really have a surface, per se. The temp and pressure at that depth are such that the atmosphere smoothly transitions into liquid without a distinct boundary.

Quoted from Wikipedia:

It is convenient to treat hydrogen as gas in the upper layer extending downward from the cloud layer to a depth of about 1,000 km, and as liquid in deeper layers. Physically, there is no clear boundary — gas smoothly becomes hotter and denser as one descends.

Yeah, you're right. That's why I used "at or near" and quoted "surface". However, unless I have really badly misunderstood the concepts involved, that's the beauty of kinetic bombardment (and its big brother, relativistic kill vehicles): the projectile doesn't have to remain solid to deal damage, a cloud of semi-molten, semi-vaporized tungsten could be just as devastating to the "bugs" we're hunting.

In any case, it is what it is. I'm not sure there's much I can do to modify it to be more reasonable-sounding without damaging the core concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That subscript "2" in L2 is doing something wonky to the spacing that's bugging me. I'm not sure how well the rods would be able to survive penetrating that deep into the atmosphere. Also, Jupiter's liquid core doesn't really have a surface, per se. The temp and pressure at that depth are such that the atmosphere smoothly transitions into liquid without a distinct boundary.

Overall, I do quite like this, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hexagonal.

'kay.

Cylindrical.

Ehhh, not in this case. Rods are actually a direct reference to real-life source material, and feels much more appropriate in this case.

A cylinder's length is not its radius. Did you take geometry?

Ouch. I was actually thinking of the radius of the spacecraft, but you're right, that sentence makes no sense.

Because nobody could thoroughly remove every trace of a system. This also tips your hand on the "nothing was alive in it, so it must be the thing itself" front.

Fair enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda had my fingers crossed that it wouldn't be time travel, still definitely deserving of an upvote though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hexagon-shaped

Hexagonal.

rod-shaped

Cylindrical.

booms approximately 25 meters in length

boom radii of 21 m

A cylinder's length is not its radius. Did you take geometry?

a complete lack of life-support capability, thus ruling out the possibility that they are or were manned satellites

Because nobody could thoroughly remove every trace of a system. This also tips your hand on the "nothing was alive in it, so it must be the thing itself" front.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot better now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the bigger issue is just an overall tense issue there, which I've adjusted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It may just be my unfamiliarity with some of the astrophysical terms, then. I may just be grumping over my lack of caffeine and adequate sleep. I should stop going to crit. articles when I'm not at 100% lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:| I could have sworn I saw them just a minute ago, but they're gone XD. Never mind that bit, then, lol.

Still sticking to the rest of my other post on this chain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm on board for this mission. Absolutely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't see grammar errors, though I'm slightly iffy about the addendum starting with "over the following 18 months" - it should in itself state over the following months from when, even if it's censored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. Fixed, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
transvestite gnome in drag who orchestrates the attacks of the satellites through mime performances

What. Where on Earth (or Jupiter) did it it say that? :|

E: I make no typos ever.

E2: And I mean ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. Short, sweet, and with a neat punchline.

I also fail to see the lack of layman's terminology - IMHO the article is adequately written for what it poses as. SCP Foundation doesn't engage in sesquipedalian loquaciousness but neither do they talking in Simple English..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What grammar errors would those be, pray tell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
following the detection of anomalous high-frequency radio signals of artificial original by Foundation interplanetary probe [REDACTED] near Io.

Is that meant to be "origin"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreeing with what Roget said. I didn't particularly find anything engaging here, and the little grammar errors and lack of layman's terminology made me twitch.

Also, it's a generic "message from a bad past/future/alternate timeline."

On a sidenote, this is somehow spurring me to take up my "The Drunktext of Chthulhu" idea again, so thanks for that.

All in all, neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clever. I like it.

And I would agree with Garrey's suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A newbie with helpful nitpicks! It followed me home, can I keep it?

Fixed, thanks. <3

Edit: Really? I thought that the particular style of mimetic performance was actually fairly well suited for the theoretical —

Waaait a minute, I see what you did there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a nit really, but the addendum that explains how it's a transvestite gnome in drag who orchestrates the attacks of the satellites through mime performances might be a little OTT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I ended up reading the log first (I'm not sure why, in retrospect), and I thought it was funny. However, I couldn't get myself to read through the description afterwards, because the entire idea is pretty much encapsulated in the log, and so I can see where Roget's coming from - I'm not sure if I'd have found it as funny if I'd read through the rest of the article first. But, anyway, I liked it, so +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I see what you were going for here, but the log at the end just left me with a "that's it?" The final conclusion of the writing did nothing for me. Well written, but just not my cup o' tea.
